title: Nearby Search (Yelp / Maps) — System Design
description: Design a proximity-search service: given a user's location, return the closest matching places (restaurants, shops) fast. The core problem is indexing 2D geography so 'find everything within radius R' is a cheap lookup instead of a full scan.

nodes.label: Mobile / Web Client
nodes.description: User searching for places near a location.

nodes.label: Load Balancer / API GW
nodes.description: Routes and rate-limits incoming search and write requests.

nodes.label: Nearby Search Service
nodes.description: Resolves a lat/lng + radius into nearby place ids, then ranks.

nodes.label: Geo Index
nodes.description: Spatial index (geohash / quadtree / S2) mapping cells to place ids.

nodes.label: Place Service
nodes.description: CRUD and reads of place details/metadata.

nodes.label: Place Store
nodes.description: Authoritative place records (name, location, category, hours).

nodes.label: Place Cache
nodes.description: Hot place details hydrated into search results.

nodes.label: Ranking Service
nodes.description: Scores candidates by distance, rating, popularity, and filters.

nodes.label: Index Builder
nodes.description: Consumes place changes and updates the geo index cells.

nodes.label: Place Change Queue
nodes.description: Streams place create/update/delete events to the indexer.

nodes.label: Routing / ETA Service
nodes.description: Optional: computes travel distance/ETA for ranking and display.

functional: Given a location (lat/lng) and radius, return places within it.
Rank results by a blend of distance, rating, and popularity.
Add / update / remove places; results reflect changes within minutes.
Return place details (name, address, hours, rating) for results.
nonFunctional: Read-heavy: searches vastly outnumber place updates.
Low latency: search p99 < 200 ms including ranking.
Scale to ~100M places worldwide and high query volume in dense cities.
Highly available for search; place updates can be eventually consistent.
Correct geography: must handle radius queries that cross index-cell boundaries.

capacity.label: Places
capacity.note: Global; very dense in cities, sparse elsewhere.

capacity.label: Searches per second
capacity.note: Spiky around meal times and events.

capacity.label: Place updates
capacity.note: Far below reads; indexing can be async.

capacity.label: Typical radius
capacity.note: Drives how many index cells a query touches.

capacity.label: Result set
capacity.note: Ranked subset, not everything in radius.

api.description: Find ranked places near a location within a radius, with optional filters.
Create or update a place. Emits a change event that updates the geo index asynchronously.
Fetch full details for one place.

dataModel.name: geo_index
dataModel.note: Derived: cell key -> place ids. Backed by a spatial structure.

fields.name: cell
fields.type: varchar (geohash prefix / S2 cell id) PK

patterns.name: Geospatial indexing
patterns.what: Map 2D coordinates to a 1D cell key (geohash/S2) or tree so radius queries touch a few cells, not all rows.
patterns.whenToUse: Any 'find things near a point' query at scale.

patterns.name: Cell neighbor expansion
patterns.what: Query the target cell plus its neighbors so results near a cell boundary aren't missed.
patterns.whenToUse: Grid/geohash indexes where a radius can straddle cell edges.

patterns.name: Adaptive cell size
patterns.what: Use finer cells in dense areas and coarser in sparse ones (tree-based or precision tuning).
patterns.whenToUse: Highly non-uniform spatial density (cities vs countryside).

patterns.name: Candidate generation + ranking
patterns.what: Cheaply gather candidates from the index, then rank/filter the smaller set.
patterns.whenToUse: When the exact, ranked result is expensive but a recall-y candidate set is cheap.

patterns.name: Async index update via change stream
patterns.what: Persist writes, emit change events, and update the derived index off the write path.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy systems where the index can lag writes by minutes.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Scan and Compute Distance
description: Start simply: store places with lat/lng, and on a search compute the distance from the query point to every place, keep those within the radius.
Correct, but it's a full table scan per query — O(all places) for every search. At 100M places and 10K searches/sec this is hopeless.
Even a bounding-box filter on lat/lng (two range predicates) helps only modestly and indexes poorly on two independent dimensions.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why doesn't a normal B-tree index on (lat, lng) make radius search fast?
whyNow: Establishing why naive distance scans and 2D range indexes fail is what motivates a real spatial index.

recap.newRisk: Full scan per query — won't scale at all.

traps.trap: Indexing lat and lng separately and intersecting ranges.
traps.why: A bounding box still returns a huge strip; two 1D indexes don't compose into 2D locality.
traps.instead: Map 2D space to a locality-preserving 1D cell key (geohash) or a spatial tree.

steps.title: 2. Geospatial Index: Geohash / S2 Cells
description: Divide the globe into a grid of cells and give each a key. Geohash interleaves lat/lng bits into a string whose prefix length sets precision; S2 maps the sphere to a quadtree of cells. Nearby points share a cell-key prefix.
Index each place by its cell key: cell -> set of place ids. A radius query computes which cells cover the area and unions their place lists — touching a handful of cells, not 100M rows.
Precompute and store each place's geohash so writes index cheaply and reads are prefix lookups.
steps.decisionPrompt: Geohash vs S2 vs a quadtree — what tradeoffs would push you to each?

concepts.definition: Interleave latitude/longitude bits into a base-32 string; prefix length controls cell size.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns 2D proximity into 1D prefix matching, which any KV/string index handles well.
concepts.example: All places in a ~150m cell share a 7-char geohash prefix.

concepts.definition: Hierarchical decomposition of space into nested cells with stable ids.
concepts.whyItMatters: Adapts cell granularity and handles sphere geometry better than a flat grid.
concepts.example: Query a covering set of S2 cells at the right level for the radius.

whyNow: The spatial index is the heart of the problem; choosing and justifying geohash/S2 is the central senior signal.

recap.after: Radius search reads a few cells' place lists.
recap.newRisk: A circle doesn't align to square cells — boundaries and false positives need handling.

steps.title: 3. Handle Cell Boundaries and the Radius
description: A search circle rarely sits inside one cell. Query the user's cell plus its 8 neighbors so a place just across a boundary isn't missed.
Cells are squares and the query is a circle, so the cell union over-covers: after gathering candidates, do an exact distance filter (haversine) to drop those actually outside the radius.
Pick the cell precision so a few cells cover the typical radius — too coarse pulls in too many candidates, too fine means querying many cells.
steps.decisionPrompt: A cafe is 50m away but in the cell next door. How do you make sure it's returned?

concepts.definition: Including adjacent cells in a spatial query to cover boundary-straddling results.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, results near a cell edge are silently dropped.
concepts.example: Query target cell + its 8 neighbors, then exact-distance filter.

whyNow: The boundary problem is the classic follow-up; addressing it proves you understand the index isn't a magic 'within radius' oracle.

recap.after: Neighbor cells + exact filter give correct radius results.
recap.newRisk: Dense city cells can still hold huge candidate lists; sparse rural ones are nearly empty.

traps.trap: Querying only the cell the point falls in.
traps.why: Misses nearby places that happen to be in an adjacent cell.
traps.instead: Expand to neighbor cells, then exact-distance filter.

flows.name: Radius query: cover cells + neighbors + exact filter
flows.note: A search computes the covering cells plus neighbors, unions their place ids, then exact-distance filters because square cells over-cover a circular radius.

messages.label: fetch place ids for those cells

steps.title: 4. Adapt to Uneven Density
description: Place density varies wildly: a downtown cell may hold thousands of places while a rural cell holds none. A single fixed cell size is wrong everywhere.

Use variable precision: in dense areas use finer cells (longer geohash / deeper quadtree level) so candidate lists stay small; in sparse areas use coarser cells so a query still finds something nearby.
Tree structures (quadtree/S2) do this naturally by subdividing only where density is high; with geohash you adjust prefix length by region or expand outward until enough results are found.
steps.decisionPrompt: Manhattan vs rural Montana with the same cell size — what breaks in each?

concepts.definition: Varying cell granularity with local density.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps candidate counts bounded in cities while still returning results in sparse areas.
concepts.example: Quadtree subdivides a busy block into many cells; leaves a rural region as one.

whyNow: Real geography is non-uniform; handling density is what makes the index perform consistently, not just on average.

recap.after: Cell granularity tracks density; bounded candidate counts.
recap.newRisk: Variable cells complicate neighbor logic; ranking still needed to pick the best of many candidates.

steps.title: 5. Rank and Filter Candidates
description: The index gives candidates (recall); now produce the ordered result the user wants. Apply filters (category, open-now, price) first to shrink the set.
Score remaining candidates by a blend of exact distance, rating, and popularity — distance alone isn't what users want (a great cafe 400m away beats a mediocre one next door).
Hydrate full details for the top-K from a hot place cache (cache-aside to the place store) so only the results you return cost a detail lookup.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should you rank by raw distance, or something else? What signals matter?
whyNow: Separating cheap candidate generation from a richer ranking pass is the standard search architecture and keeps the index simple.

concepts.definition: A cheap recall stage that gathers candidates, then an expensive precision stage that orders them.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the hot index lookup simple while allowing rich, evolving ranking.
concepts.example: Geo index returns 500 candidates; the ranker scores and returns the top 20.

recap.newRisk: Ranking adds latency; index must stay fresh as places change.

flows.name: Candidates -> filter -> rank -> hydrate
flows.note: Cheap recall from the index, then filter, score by distance/rating/popularity, and hydrate full details for only the top-K from the place cache.

steps.title: 6. Keep the Index Fresh (Async Write Path)
description: Place writes go to the authoritative store; the place service emits a change event onto a queue. An indexer consumes events and upserts the affected geo cells.
Decoupling indexing from the write path keeps writes fast and lets the read-heavy index scale independently; the index may lag writes by minutes, which the requirements allow.
Shard the geo index by cell-key prefix (region) so dense regions live on dedicated shards; replicate read replicas for query volume.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why update the geo index asynchronously instead of synchronously on each place write?
patterns: Async index update via change stream
whyNow: Tying the write path to async indexing closes the loop: reads stay fast, the index scales separately, and freshness is a tunable lag.

recap.after: Index updated off the write path; sharded by region; eventually fresh.
recap.newRisk: Index lag; a move re-keys a place across cells (delete-from-old + add-to-new must be atomic-ish).

bottlenecks.issue: A dense-region index shard gets hammered by city queries.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Finer cells + dedicated shard + read replicas for hot regions.

bottlenecks.issue: A place moving location lingers in its old cell.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Indexer removes from the old cell and adds to the new on each change event.

failureDrills.scenario: The indexer falls behind during a bulk import.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads serve slightly stale results; the queue absorbs the backlog and catches up.
failureDrills.mitigation: Eventually-consistent index + scalable consumers; bound the acceptable lag.

flows.name: Place change -> async geo-index update
flows.note: A write persists to the authoritative store and emits a change event; the indexer re-keys the place across geo cells off the write path.

messages.label: remove old cell, add new cell

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Geo-Indexed Nearby Search
finalDesign.description: Places persist to an authoritative store and emit change events; an indexer maintains a sharded geospatial index (geohash/S2) mapping cells to place ids. A search resolves a location+radius into a covering set of cells (plus neighbors), gathers candidate place ids, exact-distance filters, ranks by distance/rating/popularity, and hydrates the top-K from a hot place cache. Cell granularity adapts to density; indexing is async so reads stay fast.

functional.how: Covering cells + neighbor expansion + exact-distance filter.
Filters applied before ranking on the candidate set.
Ranking service blends signals over candidates.
Change events update the geo index asynchronously.

nonFunctional.how: Few-cell index lookup + bounded candidates + cached details.
Index sharded by region; read replicas for query volume.
Adaptive cell granularity bounds candidate counts everywhere.
Read path independent of writes; eventually-consistent index.

say: Confirm it's read-heavy proximity search with ranking, not exact GIS routing.
Agree the index may lag writes by minutes (eventual consistency).
Show the naive scan to motivate a spatial index.
Handle boundaries with neighbor cells + exact filter.
Async index maintenance via change stream; shard by region.
Tradeoffs: cell-size tuning, index lag, dense-region hotspots.

expectations: May miss boundary and density issues.
Handles boundaries with neighbor expansion + exact filter.
Reasons about adaptive precision and hot-region sharding.
Discusses geohash vs S2 vs quadtree tradeoffs.
Extends cleanly to moving objects and ETA-aware ranking.

followUps: How would you extend this to moving objects (e.g. nearby drivers updating location every few seconds)?
Geohash vs S2 vs quadtree — when would you pick each in production?
How do you handle a place that changes location (re-keying across cells)?
How would you add ETA-aware ranking using a routing service without blowing the latency budget?
How do you keep dense-city index shards from becoming hotspots?
